```plaintext
Title: Resolve Time-Bound Risk - Unencrypted Secrets in Dev Environment
Description:
- Reason for acceptance: Development environment is isolated and monitored.
- Mitigation plan: Encrypt secrets before moving to production.
- Resolution deadline: 2023-12-31
```

#### Step 2: Set Up Automated Reminders

Use GitHub Actions or Jira's automation features to set up reminders.

```yaml
name: Send Reminder for Accepted Risks

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 * * *' # Run daily at 9 AM

jobs:
  send-reminder:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Send Reminder for Accepted Risks
      run: |
        jira_issue_key="DEVSEC-123"
        today=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
        
        # Check if the issue is still open and within the deadline
        jql_query="issue=$jira_issue_key AND resolution = Unresolved AND dueDate > $today"
        response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")
        
        if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
          echo "Reminder: Issue $jira_issue_key is still open and within the deadline."
          
          # Send notification to relevant teams
          curl -X POST https://your-slack-webhook-url \
            -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
            -d '{"text": "Reminder: Resolve time-bound risk for issue DEVSEC-123 by 2023-12-31."}'
        fi
```

#### Step 3: Integrate with CI/CD Pipeline

Ensure that the pipeline checks for open issues before proceeding with deployments.

```yaml
name: Build, Scan, Validate, Deploy Image with Compliance Checks

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Set up QEMU (for multi-arch builds)
      uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v2
    
    - name: Set up Docker Buildx
      uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v1

    - name: Log in to ECR
      uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v1
      
    - name: Build and push Docker image
      run: |
        docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .
        docker tag myapp <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        docker push <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest

    - name: Install Trivy
      run: |
        curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.24.1
      
    - name: trivy-scan
      run: |
        docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        trivy --exit-code=1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL image <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest > trivy_report.txt

    - name: Validate Terraform Compliance Tags
      run: |
        terraform show | grep 'Compliance = "SOC2"' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required compliance tag"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate Secrets References
      run: |
        kubectl get secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required secret reference"; exit 1; }
        
        aws secretsmanager list-secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required AWS Secrets Manager secret reference"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access
      run: |
        aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name ci-build-user | grep 'CI-Build-Role' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required CI role"; exit 1; }
        
        kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ci-deploy-user > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required cluster access permissions for ci-deploy-user"; exit 1; }

    - name: Check Open Jira Issues
      run: |
        jql_query="issue in (DEVSEC-123) AND resolution = Unresolved"
        response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")
        
        if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
          echo "Open Jira issue found: $jql_query"
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Upload Trivy Report
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v2
      with:
        name: trivy-scan-report
        path: trivy_report.txt
      
    - name: Deploy to EKS
      run: |
        kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
```

### Summary

By following these steps, you can effectively manage time-bound accepted risks in your CI/CD pipeline:

1. **Document the Risk:** Clearly document why the risk is accepted and when it needs to be resolved.
2. **Create a Jira Ticket or Issue Tracker Entry:** Track resolution of the risk using an issue tracker.
3. **Automate Reminders and Notifications:** Set up automated reminders to notify stakeholders about upcoming deadlines.
4. **Integrate with CI/CD Pipeline:** Ensure that the pipeline checks for open issues before proceeding with deployments.

This approach ensures that time-bound risks are monitored, addressed within specified timelines, and integrated seamlessly into your CI/CD workflow.

turn_logs.user: C2 update: platform moved permissions to workflow-level block, so jobs no longer need their own contents: read entries.
turn_logs.response:
Great! Moving permissions to the workflow-level block can simplify your GitHub Actions workflows by centralizing access controls. This change ensures that all jobs within a workflow inherit the necessary permissions without needing to specify them individually.

### Updated Workflow Example

Here’s an example of how you might structure your GitHub Actions workflow with centralized permissions at the workflow level:

```yaml
name: Build, Scan, Validate, Deploy Image with Compliance Checks

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

permissions:
  contents: read  # Centralized permission for all jobs to read repository contents

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Set up QEMU (for multi-arch builds)
      uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v2
    
    - name: Set up Docker Buildx
      uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v1

    - name: Log in to ECR
      uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v1
      
    - name: Build and push Docker image
      run: |
        docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .
        docker tag myapp <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        docker push <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest

    - name: Install Trivy
      run: |
        curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.24.1
      
    - name: trivy-scan
      run: |
        docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        trivy --exit-code=1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL image <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest > trivy_report.txt

    - name: Validate Terraform Compliance Tags
      run: |
        terraform show | grep 'Compliance = "SOC2"' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required compliance tag"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate Secrets References
      run: |
        kubectl get secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required secret reference"; exit 1; }
        
        aws secretsmanager list-secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required AWS Secrets Manager secret reference"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access
      run: |
        aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name ci-build-user | grep 'CI-Build-Role' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required CI role"; exit 1; }
        
        kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ci-deploy-user > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required cluster access permissions for ci-deploy-user"; exit 1; }

    - name: Check Open Jira Issues
      run: |
        jql_query="issue in (DEVSEC-123) AND resolution = Unresolved"
        response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")
        
        if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
          echo "Open Jira issue found: $jql_query"
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Upload Trivy Report
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v2
      with:
        name: trivy-scan-report
        path: trivy_report.txt
      
    - name: Deploy to EKS
      run: |
        kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
```

### Explanation

1. **Permissions Block**:
   - The `permissions` block at the top of the workflow file sets permissions for all jobs within this workflow.
   - In this example, we set `contents: read`, which allows all jobs to read repository contents.

2. **Jobs and Steps**:
   - Each job in the workflow can now rely on the centralized permissions without needing to specify them individually.
   - This simplifies the configuration and ensures consistency across all jobs within the workflow.

### Benefits

- **Simplified Configuration**: Centralizing permissions reduces redundancy and makes your workflows easier to maintain.
- **Consistency**: Ensures that all jobs in a given workflow have consistent access controls.
- **Security**: Helps prevent accidental over-permissions by centralizing control.

By moving permissions to the workflow-level block, you can streamline your CI/CD pipeline while maintaining robust security practices.

turn_logs.user: Let us define an incident response runbook for compromised container image detection.
turn_logs.response:
Creating an incident response runbook for detecting and responding to compromised container images is crucial for ensuring rapid mitigation of security threats. Here’s a detailed outline of the steps involved in such a runbook:

### Incident Response Runbook: Compromised Container Image Detection

#### 1. **Detection**
   - **Monitoring Tools**: Use tools like AWS CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and Amazon Inspector to monitor for suspicious activities.
   - **Alerting Mechanisms**: Set up alerts to notify the security team when a compromised image is detected.

#### 2. **Initial Response**
   - **Notification**: Notify the incident response team via Slack, email, or SMS.
   - **Documentation**: Document the time and details of the detection for further analysis.

#### 3. **Containment**
   - **Stop Deployment**: Immediately stop any ongoing deployments using the compromised image.
     ```sh
     kubectl rollout undo deployment/<deployment-name>
     ```
   - **Isolate Environment**: Isolate the affected environment to prevent further spread of the threat.
     ```sh
     kubectl cordon <node-name>
     ```

#### 4. **Investigation**
   - **Gather Evidence**:
     - Collect logs from CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and other monitoring tools.
     - Retrieve container image details from ECR or Docker Hub.
     - Review Kubernetes events and audit logs for suspicious activities.

   - **Analyze Logs**: Use SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) tools to analyze the collected data.
   
   - **Identify Attack Vector**:
     - Determine how the image was compromised (e.g., unauthorized access, supply chain attack).
     - Identify any vulnerabilities or misconfigurations that allowed the compromise.

#### 5. **Remediation**
   - **Remove Compromised Image**: Delete the compromised container image from your registry.
     ```sh
     docker rmi <image-id>
     ```

   - **Update Secrets and Credentials**:
     - Regenerate any secrets or credentials used by the compromised image.
     - Update AWS Secrets Manager, Kubernetes secrets, etc.

   - **Deploy Clean Image**: Deploy a clean version of the container image from a trusted source.
     ```sh
     kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
     ```

#### 6. **Recovery**
   - **Restore Environment**:
     - Restore any affected systems or services using backups or snapshots.
     - Re-enable nodes that were cordoned during containment.

   - **Monitor for Recurrence**: Continue monitoring the environment to ensure no further compromise occurs.
   
#### 7. **Post-Incident Review**
   - **Review Incident Response Process**:
     - Evaluate the effectiveness of the incident response process.
     - Identify any gaps or areas for improvement in your security practices.

   - **Update Documentation and Policies**:
     - Update your runbook based on lessons learned from this incident.
     - Revise security policies to prevent similar incidents in the future.

### Example Runbook Workflow

#### Step-by-Step Guide

1. **Detection**
    ```plaintext
    Alert: Compromised Container Image Detected (Image ID: <image-id>)
    ```

2. **Initial Response**
    ```sh
    # Notify Security Team
    curl -X POST https://your-slack-webhook-url \
      -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
      -d '{"text": "Alert: Compromised Container Image Detected (Image ID: <image-id>)"}'
    
    # Document the Incident
    echo "$(date): Alert received for compromised container image" >> incident-log.txt
    ```

3. **Containment**
    ```sh
    # Stop Deployment
    kubectl rollout undo deployment/<deployment-name>
    
    # Isolate Environment
    kubectl cordon <node-name>
    ```

4. **Investigation**
    ```plaintext
    - Gather Logs from CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and Kubernetes.
    - Analyze logs using SIEM tools to identify attack vectors.
    ```

5. **Remediation**
    ```sh
    # Remove Compromised Image
    docker rmi <image-id>
    
    # Update Secrets
    aws secretsmanager update-secret --secret-id my-app-secret --secret-string '{"username":"myuser","password":"new_password"}'
    
    # Deploy Clean Image
    kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
    ```

6. **Recovery**
    ```sh
    # Restore Environment
    kubectl uncordon <node-name>
    
    # Monitor for Recurrence
    aws cloudtrail lookup-events --lookup-attributes '[{"AttributeKey":"EventName","AttributeValue":"CreateImage"}]'
    ```

7. **Post-Incident Review**
    ```plaintext
    - Evaluate incident response process.
    - Update runbook and security policies based on lessons learned.
    ```

### Summary

By following this incident response runbook, you can effectively detect, contain, investigate, remediate, and recover from compromised container images:

1. **Detection**: Use monitoring tools to detect suspicious activities.
2. **Initial Response**: Notify the team and document the incident.
3. **Containment**: Stop deployments and isolate affected environments.
4. **Investigation**: Gather evidence and analyze logs to identify attack vectors.
5. **Remediation**: Remove compromised images, update secrets, and deploy clean images.
6. **Recovery**: Restore the environment and monitor for recurrence.
7. **Post-Incident Review**: Evaluate response process and update documentation.

This runbook ensures a structured approach to handling compromised container images, minimizing damage and preventing future incidents.

turn_logs.user: Can PagerDuty be auto-triggered from both GuardDuty and failed security gates?
turn_logs.response:
Yes, you can set up PagerDuty to automatically trigger alerts based on events from AWS GuardDuty and failed security gates in your CI/CD pipeline. This integration ensures that your incident response team is promptly notified when potential threats or security issues are detected.

### Setting Up Auto-Triggering from GuardDuty

1. **Enable GuardDuty Findings Integration with SNS:**
   - Configure AWS GuardDuty to send findings to an Amazon SNS topic.
   
2. **Set Up SNS Topic for PagerDuty Alerts:**
   - Create an SNS topic and subscribe your PagerDuty service to this topic.

3. **Configure GuardDuty to Publish Findings to SNS:**
   ```sh
   aws guardduty create-archived-findings-filter --detector-id <DETECTOR_ID> \
     --name "CriticalFindings" \
     --findingCriteria '{"Criterion": {"severity": {"Gte": 4}}}' \
     --action "Publish" \
     --publishFrequency "FIFTEEN_MINUTES"
   ```

### Example GuardDuty to SNS Integration

1. **Create an SNS Topic:**
   ```sh
   aws sns create-topic --name guardduty-findings
   ```

2. **Subscribe PagerDuty Service to the SNS Topic:**
   - Use the PagerDuty API or UI to subscribe your service to the SNS topic.

3. **Configure GuardDuty Finding Filter and Action:**
   ```sh
   aws guardduty create-archived-findings-filter --detector-id <DETECTOR_ID> \
     --name "CriticalFindings" \
     --findingCriteria '{"Criterion": {"severity": {"Gte": 4}}}' \
     --action "Publish" \
     --publishFrequency "FIFTEEN_MINUTES"
   ```

### Setting Up Auto-Triggering from Failed Security Gates

1. **Integrate GitHub Actions with PagerDuty:**
   - Use the `pagerduty/pagerduty-webhook` action to send alerts when security gates fail.

2. **Example GitHub Action for Failed Security Checks:**

```yaml
name: Build, Scan, Validate, Deploy Image with Compliance Checks

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Set up QEMU (for multi-arch builds)
      uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v2
    
    - name: Set up Docker Buildx
      uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v1

    - name: Log in to ECR
      uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v1
      
    - name: Build and push Docker image
      run: |
        docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .
        docker tag myapp <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        docker push <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest

    - name: Install Trivy
      run: |
        curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.24.1
      
    - name: trivy-scan
      run: |
        docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        trivy --exit-code=1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL image <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest > trivy_report.txt

    - name: Validate Terraform Compliance Tags
      run: |
        terraform show | grep 'Compliance = "SOC2"' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required compliance tag"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate Secrets References
      run: |
        kubectl get secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required secret reference"; exit 1; }
        
        aws secretsmanager list-secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required AWS Secrets Manager secret reference"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access
      run: |
        aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name ci-build-user | grep 'CI-Build-Role' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required CI role"; exit 1; }
        
        kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ci-deploy-user > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required cluster access permissions for ci-deploy-user"; exit 1; }

    - name: Check Open Jira Issues
      run: |
        jql_query="issue in (DEVSEC-123) AND resolution = Unresolved"
        response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")
        
        if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
          echo "Open Jira issue found: $jql_query"
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Upload Trivy Report
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v2
      with:
        name: trivy-scan-report
        path: trivy_report.txt
      
    - name: Deploy to EKS
      run: |
        kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

    - name: Send PagerDuty Alert on Failure
      if: ${{ failure() }}
      uses: pagerduty/pagerduty-webhook@v1
      with:
        service_key: ${{ secrets.PAGERDUTY_SERVICE_KEY }}
        description: 'Security gate failed in CI/CD pipeline'
        details: |
          - Build ID: ${GITHUB_RUN_ID}
          - Job Name: ${GITHUB_JOB}
          - Failure Reason: Security checks failed
```

### Summary

By setting up auto-triggering from both GuardDuty and failed security gates, you can ensure that your incident response team is promptly notified of potential threats or security issues:

1. **GuardDuty to SNS Integration**:
   - Configure GuardDuty to send findings to an SNS topic.
   - Subscribe PagerDuty service to the SNS topic.

2. **Failed Security Gates in CI/CD Pipeline**:
   - Use GitHub Actions to trigger a PagerDuty alert when security gates fail.

This setup ensures that your team is notified of critical issues in real-time, enabling rapid response and mitigation.

turn_logs.user: What alert noise controls do you recommend so on-call is not overwhelmed?
turn_logs.response:
Managing alert noise is crucial to ensure that on-call teams are not overwhelmed with irrelevant or low-priority alerts. Here are several strategies to control alert noise while still maintaining effective monitoring:

### 1. **Threshold-Based Alerts**
Configure thresholds for different severity levels and only trigger high-severity alerts.

#### Example:
- **High Severity**: Only send alerts if the severity level is critical.
- **Medium Severity**: Send alerts but with lower frequency (e.g., hourly instead of real-time).

```yaml
aws guardduty create-threat-intel-set --detector-id <DETECTOR_ID> \
  --name "CriticalThreats" \
  --threatintelset-body '{"severity": {"Gte": 4}}'
```

### 2. **Aggregation and Deduplication**
Aggregate multiple similar alerts into a single notification to reduce noise.

#### Example:
- Use AWS CloudWatch Alarms with `treat_missing_data` set to `breaching`.

```yaml
resources:
  - Type: "AWS::CloudWatch::Alarm"
    Properties:
      AlarmName: "HighSeverityAlerts"
      MetricName: "FindingCount"
      Namespace: "GuardDuty"
      Statistic: "Sum"
      Period: 300
      EvaluationPeriods: 1
      Threshold: 4
      ComparisonOperator: "GreaterThanThreshold"
      TreatMissingData: breaching
```

### 3. **Scheduled Alerts**
Schedule alerts to run at specific times to avoid overwhelming on-call teams during off-hours.

#### Example:
- Use AWS EventBridge to trigger scheduled alerts.

```yaml
resources:
  - Type: "AWS::Events::Rule"
    Properties:
      Name: "DailySecurityReview"
      ScheduleExpression: "cron(0 8 * * ? *)"
      Targets:
        - Arn: "<SNS_TOPIC_ARN>"
          Id: "Target1"
```

### 4. **Contextual Alerts**
Provide additional context in alerts to help on-call teams quickly understand the issue.

#### Example:
- Include relevant metadata and links to logs or dashboards.

```yaml
resources:
  - Type: "AWS::SNS::Topic"
    Properties:
      TopicName: "SecurityAlerts"
      Subscription:
        - Protocol: email
          Endpoint: "<EMAIL>"
```

### 5. **Dynamic Thresholds**
Adjust alert thresholds based on historical data and current conditions.

#### Example:
- Use machine learning models to dynamically adjust thresholds.

```yaml
resources:
  - Type: "AWS::SageMaker::Model"
    Properties:
      ModelName: "ThresholdAdjuster"
      ExecutionRoleArn: "<ROLE_ARN>"
```

### 6. **Alert Suppression**
Suppress alerts for known issues or during maintenance windows.

#### Example:
- Use AWS Lambda to suppress alerts based on conditions.

```yaml
resources:
  - Type: "AWS::Lambda::Function"
    Properties:
      FunctionName: "AlertSuppression"
      Handler: index.handler
      Role: "<ROLE_ARN>"
      Code:
        ZipFile: |
          import json
          def handler(event, context):
              if event['detail']['findingType'] == 'KnownIssue':
                  return {'suppress': True}
              else:
                  return {'suppress': False}
```

### 7. **Alert Prioritization**
Prioritize alerts based on severity and impact.

#### Example:
- Use a tiered alerting system where critical issues are escalated immediately, while lower-severity issues are grouped or delayed.

```yaml
resources:
  - Type: "AWS::SNS::Topic"
    Properties:
      TopicName: "CriticalAlerts"
      Subscription:
        - Protocol: email
          Endpoint: "<EMAIL>"
```

### Example GitHub Actions Workflow for Alert Suppression

Here’s an example of how you might set up alert suppression in a GitHub Actions workflow:

```yaml
name: Build, Scan, Validate, Deploy Image with Compliance Checks and Alert Suppression

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Set up QEMU (for multi-arch builds)
      uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v2
    
    - name: Set up Docker Buildx
      uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v1

    - name: Log in to ECR
      uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v1
      
    - name: Build and push Docker image
      run: |
        docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .
        docker tag myapp <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        docker push <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest

    - name: Install Trivy
      run: |
        curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.24.1
      
    - name: trivy-scan
      run: |
        docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        trivy --exit-code=1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL image <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest > trivy_report.txt

    - name: Validate Terraform Compliance Tags
      run: |
        terraform show | grep 'Compliance = "SOC2"' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required compliance tag"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate Secrets References
      run: |
        kubectl get secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required secret reference"; exit 1; }
        
        aws secretsmanager list-secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required AWS Secrets Manager secret reference"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access
      run: |
        aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name ci-build-user | grep 'CI-Build-Role' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required CI role"; exit 1; }
        
        kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ci-deploy-user > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required cluster access permissions for ci-deploy-user"; exit 1; }

    - name: Check Open Jira Issues
      run: |
        jql_query="issue in (DEVSEC-123) AND resolution = Unresolved"
        response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")
        
        if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
          echo "Open Jira issue found: $jql_query"
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Upload Trivy Report
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v2
      with:
        name: trivy-scan-report
        path: trivy_report.txt
      
    - name: Deploy to EKS
      run: |
        kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

    - name: Send PagerDuty Alert on Failure
      if: ${{ failure() }}
      uses: pagerduty/pagerduty-webhook@v1
      with:
        service_key: ${{ secrets.PAGERDUTY_SERVICE_KEY }}
        description: 'Security gate failed in CI/CD pipeline'
        details: |
          - Build ID: ${GITHUB_RUN_ID}
          - Job Name: ${GITHUB_JOB}
          - Failure Reason: Security checks failed

    - name: Suppress Alerts During Maintenance
      if: ${{ env.MAINTENANCE_MODE == 'true' }}
      run: |
        echo "Suppressing alerts during maintenance..."
```

### Summary

By implementing these strategies, you can effectively manage alert noise and ensure that on-call teams are not overwhelmed:

1. **Threshold-Based Alerts**: Configure thresholds for different severity levels.
2. **Aggregation and Deduplication**: Aggregate similar alerts into a single notification.
3. **Scheduled Alerts**: Schedule alerts to run at specific times.
4. **Contextual Alerts**: Provide additional context in alerts.
5. **Dynamic Thresholds**: Adjust alert thresholds based on historical data.
6. **Alert Suppression**: Suppress alerts for known issues or during maintenance windows.
7. **Alert Prioritization**: Prioritize alerts based on severity and impact.

These practices help ensure that your incident response team receives only relevant and actionable alerts, improving their efficiency and effectiveness in managing security incidents.

turn_logs.user: C3 is stricter now: trivy-scan must run with --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL so builds hard-fail on serious vulnerabilities.
turn_logs.response:
Certainly! To ensure that your CI/CD pipeline fails the build if `trivy` detects any high or critical severity vulnerabilities, you need to adjust the `trivy-scan` step in your GitHub Actions workflow accordingly.

Here’s an updated example of how you can set up this stricter requirement:

### Updated Workflow Example

```yaml
name: Build, Scan, Validate, Deploy Image with Compliance Checks and Alert Suppression

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Set up QEMU (for multi-arch builds)
      uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v2
    
    - name: Set up Docker Buildx
      uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v1

    - name: Log in to ECR
      uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v1
      
    - name: Build and push Docker image
      run: |
        docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .
        docker tag myapp <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        docker push <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest

    - name: Install Trivy
      run: |
        curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.24.1
      
    - name: trivy-scan
      run: |
        docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        if ! trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest; then
          echo "High or critical vulnerabilities detected. Build will fail."
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Validate Terraform Compliance Tags
      run: |
        terraform show | grep 'Compliance = "SOC2"' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required compliance tag"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate Secrets References
      run: |
        kubectl get secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required secret reference"; exit 1; }
        
        aws secretsmanager list-secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required AWS Secrets Manager secret reference"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access
      run: |
        aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name ci-build-user | grep 'CI-Build-Role' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required CI role"; exit 1; }
        
        kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ci-deploy-user > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required cluster access permissions for ci-deploy-user"; exit 1; }

    - name: Check Open Jira Issues
      run: |
        jql_query="issue in (DEVSEC-123) AND resolution = Unresolved"
        response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")
        
        if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
          echo "Open Jira issue found: $jql_query"
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Upload Trivy Report
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v2
      with:
        name: trivy-scan-report
        path: trivy_report.txt
      
    - name: Deploy to EKS
      run: |
        kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

    - name: Send PagerDuty Alert on Failure
      if: ${{ failure() }}
      uses: pagerduty/pagerduty-webhook@v1
      with:
        service_key: ${{ secrets.PAGERDUTY_SERVICE_KEY }}
        description: 'Security gate failed in CI/CD pipeline'
        details: |
          - Build ID: ${GITHUB_RUN_ID}
          - Job Name: ${GITHUB_JOB}
          - Failure Reason: Security checks failed

    - name: Suppress Alerts During Maintenance
      if: ${{ env.MAINTENANCE_MODE == 'true' }}
      run: |
        echo "Suppressing alerts during maintenance..."
```

### Explanation of Changes

1. **Trivy Scan with Exit Code and Severity**:
   - The `trivy-scan` step now includes the `--exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL` options.
   - If any high or critical vulnerabilities are detected, the build will fail immediately.

```yaml
- name: trivy-scan
  run: |
    docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
    if ! trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest; then
      echo "High or critical vulnerabilities detected. Build will fail."
      exit 1
    fi
```

### Summary

By enforcing the `--exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL` options in your `trivy-scan`, you ensure that any high or critical security issues will cause the build to hard-fail immediately. This helps maintain a higher level of security and ensures that only images without serious vulnerabilities are deployed.

This approach ensures that:

- **High Severity Issues**: The build fails if there are any high-severity vulnerabilities.
- **Critical Severity Issues**: The build fails if there are any critical-severity vulnerabilities.

By integrating this stricter requirement into your CI/CD pipeline, you can maintain a robust security posture and prevent the deployment of insecure images.

turn_logs.user: Please provide a final end-to-end checklist from commit to production.
turn_logs.response:
Certainly! Here’s an end-to-end checklist that covers all stages from committing code to deploying it in production while ensuring compliance with SOC2 requirements:

### End-to-End Checklist for Commit to Production

#### 1. **Commit Code**
   - Ensure the code adheres to coding standards and best practices.
   - Include unit tests and ensure they pass locally.

```sh
# Example: Run local tests
pytest .
```

#### 2. **Push Changes**
   - Push changes to a feature branch or directly to main (if using trunk-based development).

```sh
git push origin feature/my-feature-branch
```

#### 3. **Trigger CI Pipeline**
   - Ensure the commit triggers a CI pipeline that includes all necessary checks.

### CI/CD Pipeline Steps

1. **Checkout Code**

```yaml
- name: Checkout code
  uses: actions/checkout@v3
```

2. **Set Up Environment (QEMU and Docker Buildx)**

```yaml
- name: Set up QEMU (for multi-arch builds)
  uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v2
  
- name: Set up Docker Buildx
  uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v1
```

3. **Log in to Container Registry**

```yaml
- name: Log in to ECR
  uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v1
```

4. **Build and Push Docker Image**

```yaml
- name: Build and push Docker image
  run: |
    docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .
    docker tag myapp <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
    docker push <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
```

5. **Install Trivy**

```yaml
- name: Install Trivy
  run: |
    curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.24.1
```

6. **Run Trivy Scan with Exit Code and Severity**

```yaml
- name: trivy-scan
  run: |
    docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
    if ! trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest; then
      echo "High or critical vulnerabilities detected. Build will fail."
      exit 1
    fi
```

7. **Validate Terraform Compliance Tags**

```yaml
- name: Validate Terraform Compliance Tags
  run: |
    terraform show | grep 'Compliance = "SOC2"' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required compliance tag"; exit 1; }
```

8. **Validate Secrets References**

```yaml
- name: Validate Secrets References
  run: |
    kubectl get secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required secret reference"; exit 1; }

    aws secretsmanager list-secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required AWS Secrets Manager secret reference"; exit 1; }
```

9. **Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access**

```yaml
- name: Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access
  run: |
    aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name ci-build-user | grep 'CI-Build-Role' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required CI role"; exit 1; }

    kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ci-deploy-user > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required cluster access permissions for ci-deploy-user"; exit 1; }
```

10. **Check Open Jira Issues**

```yaml
- name: Check Open Jira Issues
  run: |
    jql_query="issue in (DEVSEC-123) AND resolution = Unresolved"
    response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")

    if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
      echo "Open Jira issue found: $jql_query"
      exit 1
    fi
```

11. **Upload Trivy Report**

```yaml
- name: Upload Trivy Report
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v2
  with:
    name: trivy-scan-report
    path: trivy_report.txt
```

12. **Deploy to EKS**

```yaml
- name: Deploy to EKS
  run: |
    kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
```

13. **Send PagerDuty Alert on Failure**

```yaml
- name: Send PagerDuty Alert on Failure
  if: ${{ failure() }}
  uses: pagerduty/pagerduty-webhook@v1
  with:
    service_key: ${{ secrets.PAGERDUTY_SERVICE_KEY }}
    description: 'Security gate failed in CI/CD pipeline'
    details: |
      - Build ID: ${GITHUB_RUN_ID}
      - Job Name: ${GITHUB_JOB}
      - Failure Reason: Security checks failed
```

14. **Suppress Alerts During Maintenance**

```yaml
- name: Suppress Alerts During Maintenance
  if: ${{ env.MAINTENANCE_MODE == 'true' }}
  run: |
    echo "Suppressing alerts during maintenance..."
```

### Summary of Checklist

#### Commit Code
- Ensure code adheres to coding standards.
- Include unit tests and ensure they pass locally.

#### Push Changes
- Push changes to a feature branch or main.

#### Trigger CI Pipeline
- Ensure the commit triggers a CI pipeline that includes all necessary checks.

##### CI/CD Pipeline Steps:
1. **Checkout Code**
2. **Set Up Environment (QEMU, Docker Buildx)**
3. **Log in to Container Registry**
4. **Build and Push Docker Image**
5. **Install Trivy**
6. **Run Trivy Scan with Exit Code and Severity**
7. **Validate Terraform Compliance Tags**
8. **Validate Secrets References**
9. **Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access**
10. **Check Open Jira Issues**
11. **Upload Trivy Report**
12. **Deploy to EKS**
13. **Send PagerDuty Alert on Failure**
14. **Suppress Alerts During Maintenance**

By following this checklist, you ensure that your code is thoroughly tested and validated before deployment, maintaining a high level of security and compliance with SOC2 requirements.

### Example GitHub Actions Workflow

```yaml
name: Build, Scan, Validate, Deploy Image with Compliance Checks and Alert Suppression

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Set up QEMU (for multi-arch builds)
      uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v2
    
    - name: Set up Docker Buildx
      uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v1

    - name: Log in to ECR
      uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v1
      
    - name: Build and push Docker image
      run: |
        docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .
        docker tag myapp <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        docker push <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest

    - name: Install Trivy
      run: |
        curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.24.1
      
    - name: trivy-scan
      run: |
        docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        if ! trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest; then
          echo "High or critical vulnerabilities detected. Build will fail."
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Validate Terraform Compliance Tags
      run: |
        terraform show | grep 'Compliance = "SOC2"' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required compliance tag"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate Secrets References
      run: |
        kubectl get secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required secret reference"; exit 1; }
        
        aws secretsmanager list-secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required AWS Secrets Manager secret reference"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access
      run: |
        aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name ci-build-user | grep 'CI-Build-Role' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required CI role"; exit 1; }
        
        kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ci-deploy-user > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required cluster access permissions for ci-deploy-user"; exit 1; }

    - name: Check Open Jira Issues
      run: |
        jql_query="issue in (DEVSEC-123) AND resolution = Unresolved"
        response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")
        
        if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
          echo "Open Jira issue found: $jql_query"
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Upload Trivy Report
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v2
      with:
        name: trivy-scan-report
        path: trivy_report.txt
      
    - name: Deploy to EKS
      run: |
        kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

    - name: Send PagerDuty Alert on Failure
      if: ${{ failure() }}
      uses: pagerduty/pagerduty-webhook@v1
      with:
        service_key: ${{ secrets.PAGERDUTY_SERVICE_KEY }}
        description: 'Security gate failed in CI/CD pipeline'
        details: |
          - Build ID: ${GITHUB_RUN_ID}
          - Job Name: ${GITHUB_JOB}
          - Failure Reason: Security checks failed

    - name: Suppress Alerts During Maintenance
      if: ${{ env.MAINTENANCE_MODE == 'true' }}
      run: |
        echo "Suppressing alerts during maintenance..."
```

This checklist and workflow ensure that your code is thoroughly validated, tested, and deployed in a secure manner, adhering to SOC2 compliance requirements.

turn_logs.user: Can you summarize evidence artifacts we should retain for auditors?
turn_logs.response:
Certainly! Retaining the right evidence artifacts is crucial for demonstrating compliance with SOC 2 and other regulatory requirements. Here’s a summary of key artifacts that should be retained for audits:

### Evidence Artifacts to Retain

1. **Code Repositories**
   - Commit history, including timestamps and author information.
   - Pull requests and merge approvals.

2. **Build Logs**
   - CI/CD pipeline logs showing build success/failure details.
   - Build artifacts (e.g., Docker images, binaries).

3. **Security Scans**
   - Trivy scan reports for each image version.
   - Other static code analysis and vulnerability scans.

4. **Configuration Management**
   - Terraform state files and configurations with compliance tags.
   - Infrastructure as Code (IaC) templates and their versions.

5. **Secrets Management**
   - Logs of secret creation, rotation, and deletion.
   - Audit trails for access to secrets management systems (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager).

6. **Access Control Records**
   - IAM policy changes and audit logs.
   - Kubernetes RBAC configurations and changes.

7. **Monitoring and Logging**
   - CloudTrail logs showing API activity.
   - GuardDuty findings and alerts.
   - SNS/SQS notifications for security events.

8. **Incident Response Logs**
   - Incident response documentation, including timelines and actions taken.
   - Post-incident review reports.

9. **Compliance Reports**
   - Compliance audit reports generated by tools like AWS Config or Security Hub.
   - SOC 2 compliance attestations and reports.

10. **Deployment Records**
    - Deployment logs showing successful deployments to different environments (e.g., dev, staging, prod).
    - Kubernetes deployment YAML files and their versions.

11. **Configuration Validation Logs**
    - Logs from validation steps in CI/CD pipelines.
    - Compliance tag validation results.

### Example Retention Policy

Here’s an example of how you might structure a retention policy for these artifacts:

```plaintext
Retention Policy:
- Code Repositories: Retain commit history and pull request details indefinitely.
- Build Logs: Retain logs for at least 1 year.
- Security Scans: Retain scan reports for at least 1 year.
- Configuration Management: Retain Terraform state files and IaC templates for at least 1 year.
- Secrets Management: Retain audit trails and logs for at least 1 year.
- Access Control Records: Retain IAM policy changes and Kubernetes RBAC configurations for at least 1 year.
- Monitoring and Logging: Retain CloudTrail, GuardDuty, SNS/SQS logs for at least 6 months.
- Incident Response Logs: Retain incident response documentation and post-incident review reports indefinitely.
- Compliance Reports: Retain SOC 2 compliance attestations and reports indefinitely.
- Deployment Records: Retain deployment logs and Kubernetes YAML files for at least 1 year.
- Configuration Validation Logs: Retain validation logs for at least 6 months.
```

### Summary

By retaining the following evidence artifacts, you can provide auditors with comprehensive documentation to demonstrate compliance:

1. **Code Repositories**: Commit history and pull requests.
2. **Build Logs**: CI/CD pipeline logs and build artifacts.
3. **Security Scans**: Trivy scan reports and other security scans.
4. **Configuration Management**: Terraform state files and IaC templates.
5. **Secrets Management**: Secret creation, rotation, and access audit trails.
6. **Access Control Records**: IAM policy changes and Kubernetes RBAC configurations.
7. **Monitoring and Logging**: CloudTrail, GuardDuty, SNS/SQS logs.
8. **Incident Response Logs**: Incident response documentation and post-incident reviews.
9. **Compliance Reports**: SOC 2 compliance attestations and reports.
10. **Deployment Records**: Deployment logs and Kubernetes YAML files.
11. **Configuration Validation Logs**: CI/CD validation logs.

This approach ensures that you have a comprehensive audit trail to demonstrate adherence to SOC 2 requirements and other regulatory standards.

turn_logs.user: I also need a concise runbook header template for teams.
turn_logs.response:
Certainly! A concise runbook header template is essential for ensuring consistency across different incident response documents. Here’s a template you can use:

### Incident Response Runbook Header Template

#### **Incident Name**
- **Date and Time of Detection**: [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM]
- **Incident ID**: [Unique Identifier]

#### **Summary**
- **Description**: Brief description of the incident.
- **Impact**: Description of the impact on systems, services, or users.

#### **Detection Method**
- **Tool/Service**: Name of the tool or service that detected the issue (e.g., GuardDuty, CloudTrail).
- **Alert Details**: Specific alert details and timestamps.

#### **Initial Response Actions**
1. **Notification**:
   - Notify the incident response team via Slack, email, or SMS.
2. **Documentation**:
   - Document the time and details of the detection for further analysis.

#### **Containment Steps**
1. **Stop Deployment**:
   ```sh
   kubectl rollout undo deployment/<deployment-name>
   ```
2. **Isolate Environment**:
   ```sh
   kubectl cordon <node-name>
   ```

#### **Investigation Steps**
1. **Gather Evidence**:
   - Collect logs from CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and other monitoring tools.
   - Retrieve container image details from ECR or Docker Hub.
2. **Analyze Logs**:
   - Use SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) tools to analyze the collected data.
3. **Identify Attack Vector**:
   - Determine how the incident occurred (e.g., unauthorized access, supply chain attack).
   - Identify any vulnerabilities or misconfigurations that allowed the compromise.

#### **Remediation Steps**
1. **Remove Compromised Image**:
   ```sh
   docker rmi <image-id>
   ```
2. **Update Secrets and Credentials**:
   - Regenerate any secrets or credentials used by the compromised image.
3. **Deploy Clean Image**:
   ```sh
   kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
   ```

#### **Recovery Steps**
1. **Restore Environment**:
   - Restore affected systems or services using backups or snapshots.
2. **Monitor for Recurrence**:
   - Continue monitoring the environment to ensure no further compromise occurs.

#### **Post-Incident Review**
- **Review Incident Response Process**: Evaluate the effectiveness of the incident response process.
- **Update Documentation and Policies**: Revise security policies based on lessons learned from this incident.

### Example Runbook Header

#### **Incident Name: Compromised Container Image Detection**
- **Date and Time of Detection**: 2023-10-05 14:30
- **Incident ID**: IR-2023-10-05-14-30

#### **Summary**
- **Description**: A compromised container image was detected in the production environment.
- **Impact**: Potential unauthorized access to sensitive data.

#### **Detection Method**
- **Tool/Service**: AWS GuardDuty
- **Alert Details**: Alert ID: 1234567890, Timestamp: 2023-10-05T14:25Z

#### **Initial Response Actions**
1. **Notification**:
   - Notify the security team via Slack.
2. **Documentation**:
   - Document the incident in the incident response log.

#### **Containment Steps**
1. **Stop Deployment**:
   ```sh
   kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app-deployment
   ```
2. **Isolate Environment**:
   ```sh
   kubectl cordon node-01
   ```

#### **Investigation Steps**
1. **Gather Evidence**:
   - Collect logs from CloudTrail and GuardDuty.
   - Retrieve container image details from ECR.
2. **Analyze Logs**:
   - Use SIEM tools to analyze the collected data.
3. **Identify Attack Vector**:
   - Determine that unauthorized access was through a compromised CI/CD pipeline.

#### **Remediation Steps**
1. **Remove Compromised Image**:
   ```sh
   docker rmi <image-id>
   ```
2. **Update Secrets and Credentials**:
   - Regenerate secrets in AWS Secrets Manager.
3. **Deploy Clean Image**:
   ```sh
   kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
   ```

#### **Recovery Steps**
1. **Restore Environment**:
   - Restore affected services using backups.
2. **Monitor for Recurrence**:
   - Continue monitoring the environment to ensure no further compromise.

#### **Post-Incident Review**
- **Review Incident Response Process**: Evaluate the effectiveness of the incident response process.
- **Update Documentation and Policies**: Revise security policies based on lessons learned from this incident.

This template ensures that all necessary information is captured consistently across different incidents, making it easier to respond effectively and document the process for future reference.

turn_logs.user: Final review please: confirm the mandatory controls we must not regress.
turn_logs.response:
Certainly! Here’s a final review of the mandatory controls you must maintain to ensure compliance with SOC 2 and other security requirements:

### Mandatory Controls

1. **Image Scanning**
   - Every Docker image must be scanned using `trivy` before deployment.
   - The scan step must be named exactly `trivy-scan`.
   - The build should fail if any high or critical vulnerabilities are detected.

2. **Compliance Tags in Terraform Configurations**
   - Ensure all Terraform configurations include the required compliance tag (`Compliance = "SOC2"`).

3. **Secret Management**
   - Secrets must be managed using secure methods like AWS Secrets Manager or Kubernetes secrets.
   - Validate that secret references are valid and properly managed.

4. **Access Control**
   - IAM roles and policies must adhere to least privilege principles.
   - Ensure RBAC is enabled for EKS control plane.

5. **Logging and Monitoring**
   - Enable CloudTrail to capture API activity logs.
   - Configure GuardDuty to detect potential threats in real-time.
   - Use SNS or other notification services to alert on security incidents.

6. **Resource Tagging**
   - Ensure all resources are properly tagged with necessary metadata (e.g., compliance tags).

7. **Incident Response and Documentation**
   - Maintain detailed documentation of incident response procedures.
   - Retain logs and evidence for auditing purposes, including build logs, security scan reports, access control records, and deployment records.

### Example CI/CD Workflow

Here’s an example GitHub Actions workflow that incorporates these mandatory controls:

```yaml
name: Build, Scan, Validate, Deploy Image with Compliance Checks and Alert Suppression

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Set up QEMU (for multi-arch builds)
      uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v2
    
    - name: Set up Docker Buildx
      uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v1

    - name: Log in to ECR
      uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v1
      
    - name: Build and push Docker image
      run: |
        docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .
        docker tag myapp <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        docker push <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest

    - name: Install Trivy
      run: |
        curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin v0.24.1
      
    - name: trivy-scan
      run: |
        docker pull <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest
        if ! trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/myrepo:latest; then
          echo "High or critical vulnerabilities detected. Build will fail."
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Validate Terraform Compliance Tags
      run: |
        terraform show | grep 'Compliance = "SOC2"' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required compliance tag"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate Secrets References
      run: |
        kubectl get secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required secret reference"; exit 1; }
        
        aws secretsmanager list-secrets | grep 'my-app-secret' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required AWS Secrets Manager secret reference"; exit 1; }

    - name: Validate CI Roles and Cluster Access
      run: |
        aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name ci-build-user | grep 'CI-Build-Role' > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required CI role"; exit 1; }
        
        kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ci-deploy-user > /dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "Missing required cluster access permissions for ci-deploy-user"; exit 1; }

    - name: Check Open Jira Issues
      run: |
        jql_query="issue in (DEVSEC-123) AND resolution = Unresolved"
        response=$(curl -u "$JIRA_USERNAME:$JIRA_API_TOKEN" -X GET "https://your-jira-instance.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/search?jql=${jql_query}")
        
        if [ "$(echo $response | jq '.total')" -gt 0 ]; then
          echo "Open Jira issue found: $jql_query"
          exit 1
        fi

    - name: Upload Trivy Report
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v2
      with:
        name: trivy-scan-report
        path: trivy_report.txt
      
    - name: Deploy to EKS
      run: |
        kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

    - name: Send PagerDuty Alert on Failure
      if: ${{ failure() }}
      uses: pagerduty/pagerduty-webhook@v1
      with:
        service_key: ${{ secrets.PAGERDUTY_SERVICE_KEY }}
        description: 'Security gate failed in CI/CD pipeline'
        details: |
          - Build ID: ${GITHUB_RUN_ID}
          - Job Name: ${GITHUB_JOB}
          - Failure Reason: Security checks failed

    - name: Suppress Alerts During Maintenance
      if: ${{ env.MAINTENANCE_MODE == 'true' }}
      run: |
        echo "Suppressing alerts during maintenance..."
```

### Summary of Mandatory Controls

1. **Image Scanning**
   - Ensure `trivy-scan` step is named correctly and fails on high/critical vulnerabilities.
   
2. **Compliance Tags in Terraform Configurations**
   - Validate compliance tags are present.

3. **Secret Management**
   - Verify secret references are valid and managed securely.

4. **Access Control**
   - Ensure IAM roles follow least privilege principles and RBAC is enabled for EKS control plane.

5. **Logging and Monitoring**
   - Enable CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and SNS alerts.
   
6. **Resource Tagging**
   - Ensure all resources are properly tagged with necessary metadata.

7. **Incident Response and Documentation**
   - Maintain detailed documentation of incident response procedures and retain logs/evidence for auditing purposes.

By adhering to these controls, you ensure that your CI/CD pipeline remains secure and compliant with SOC 2 requirements.

turn_logs.user: I am setting up a new AWS platform. Can you propose a clean Terraform layout for networking, EKS, and shared security controls?
How should I handle remote state for team collaboration and locking?
turn_logs.response:
Handling remote state in Terraform is crucial for team collaboration and ensuring that multiple users can safely manage infrastructure without conflicts. Here’s how you can set up remote state management using AWS S3 and DynamoDB:

### Remote State Configuration

1. **S3 Bucket**: Store the Terraform state file.
2. **DynamoDB Table**: Manage locks to prevent concurrent modifications.

#### Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket for State Storage

First, create an S3 bucket that will store your Terraform state files. You can use AWS Console or CLI commands to do this:

```sh
aws s3api create-bucket --bucket my-terraform-state-bucket --region us-east-1
```

#### Step 2: Create a DynamoDB Table for Locking

Next, create a DynamoDB table that will be used by Terraform to manage state file locks. You can use the AWS CLI or console:

```sh
aws dynamodb create-table \
    --table-name my-terraform-lock-table \
    --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \
    --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \
    --provisioned-throughput ReadCapacityUnits=5,WriteCapacityUnits=5 \
    --region us-east-1
```

#### Step 3: Configure Remote State in Terraform

Now you need to configure your `main.tf` or a separate `terraform.tfstate.d` file to use the remote backend.

```hcl
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 0.14" # Ensure compatibility with your version of Terraform

  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state-bucket"
    key            = "path/to/your/state-file.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "my-terraform-lock-table"
  }
}
```

### Example `main.tf` with Remote State Configuration

Here’s an example of how you might configure your main Terraform file to use the remote backend:

```hcl
provider "aws" {
  region = var.region
}

terraform {
  required_version = "~> 1.0"

  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state-bucket"
    key            = "path/to/your/state-file.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "my-terraform-lock-table"
  }
}

module "network" {
  source              = "./modules/network"
  vpc_cidr            = var.vpc_cidr
  public_subnets      = var.public_subnets
  private_subnets     = var.private_subnets
  azs                 = var.azs
  enable_nat_gateway  = var.enable_nat_gateway
}

module "eks" {
  source              = "./modules/eks"
  vpc_id              = module.network.vpc_id
  subnets             = module.network.public_subnets
  cluster_name        = var.cluster_name
  node_group_name     = var.node_group_name
  desired_size        = var.desired_size
}

module "security" {
  source              = "./modules/security"
  vpc_id              = module.network.vpc_id
  security_groups     = [module.eks.security_group]
}
```

### Additional Considerations

- **Permissions**: Ensure that the IAM role or user running Terraform has appropriate permissions to access S3 and DynamoDB.
  
  Example policy for S3:
  ```json
  {
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
      {
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject"],
        "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-terraform-state-bucket/path/to/your/state-file.tfstate"
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

  Example policy for DynamoDB:
  ```json
  {
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
      {
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Action": ["dynamodb:GetItem", "dynamodb:PutItem"],
        "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:us-east-1:<account-id>:table/my-terraform-lock-table"
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

- **State Versioning**: Enable versioning on your S3 bucket to keep a history of state changes.

By setting up remote state management with AWS S3 and DynamoDB, you can ensure that multiple team members can safely collaborate on Terraform configurations without conflicts.

turn_logs.user: Would you recommend Terraform workspaces or separate folders for dev, stage, and prod?
turn_logs.response:
Both Terraform workspaces and separate folders are valid approaches to managing different environments (dev, stage, prod) in a Terraform setup. Each method has its own advantages and trade-offs. Here’s an overview of both options:

### 1. **Terraform Workspaces**

**Pros:**
- **Simplicity**: You can manage multiple environments using the same set of configuration files.
- **Consistency**: The structure remains consistent across all environments, making it easier to maintain and update configurations.
- **Resource Sharing**: Resources that are common across environments (e.g., VPC) can be shared without duplication.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity in State Management**: Managing state for multiple workspaces requires careful handling of backend configuration.
- **Limited Flexibility**: If you need different settings or resources per environment, it might become cumbersome to manage within a single set of files.

#### Example Configuration

```hcl
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.0"

  # Configure the remote state backend for workspaces
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state-bucket"
    key            = "path/to/your/state-file.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "my-terraform-lock-table"
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.region
}

# Example variables for different environments
variable "region" {
  description = "The AWS region to deploy the resources."
}
variable "env" {
  description = "Environment (dev, stage, prod)"
}

module "network" {
  source              = "./modules/network"
  vpc_cidr            = var.vpc_cidr
  public_subnets      = var.public_subnets
  private_subnets     = var.private_subnets
  azs                 = var.azs
  enable_nat_gateway  = var.enable_nat_gateway
}

module "eks" {
  source              = "./modules/eks"
  vpc_id              = module.network.vpc_id
  subnets             = module.network.public_subnets
  cluster_name        = "${var.cluster_name}-${var.env}"
  node_group_name     = "${var.node_group_name}-${var.env}"
  desired_size        = var.desired_size
}

module "security" {
  source              = "./modules/security"
  vpc_id              = module.network.vpc_id
  security_groups     = [module.eks.security_group]
}
```

#### Usage

```sh
# Initialize Terraform and set up workspaces
terraform init

# Create a workspace for dev environment
terraform workspace new dev

# Apply the configuration in the dev workspace
terraform apply -var="env=dev"

# Repeat for stage and prod environments
terraform workspace new stage
terraform apply -var="env=stage"
terraform workspace new prod
terraform apply -var="env=prod"
```

### 2. **Separate Folders**

**Pros:**
- **Isolation**: Each environment has its own set of configuration files, making it easier to manage and understand.
- **Flexibility**: You can tailor configurations specifically for each environment without affecting others.

**Cons:**
- **Duplication**: Common resources might need to be duplicated across environments or managed through shared modules.
- **Complexity in Deployment**: Managing multiple sets of Terraform state files and workspaces can become complex.

#### Example Directory Structure

```
terraform-aws-platform/
├── dev/
│   ├── main.tf
│   ├── variables.tf
│   └── outputs.tf
├── stage/
│   ├── main.tf
│   ├── variables.tf
│   └── outputs.tf
└── prod/
    ├── main.tf
    ├── variables.tf
    └── outputs.tf
```

#### Example `dev/main.tf`

```hcl
provider "aws" {
  region = var.region
}

module "network" {
  source              = "../modules/network"
  vpc_cidr            = var.vpc_cidr
  public_subnets      = var.public_subnets
  private_subnets     = var.private_subnets
  azs                 = var.azs
  enable_nat_gateway  = var.enable_nat_gateway
}

module "eks" {
  source              = "../modules/eks"
  vpc_id              = module.network.vpc_id
  subnets             = module.network.public_subnets
  cluster_name        = var.cluster_name
  node_group_name     = var.node_group_name
  desired_size        = var.desired_size
}

module "security" {
  source              = "../modules/security"
  vpc_id              = module.network.vpc_id
  security_groups     = [module.eks.security_group]
}
```

#### Usage

```sh
# Initialize and apply for dev environment
cd dev/
terraform init
terraform apply

# Repeat for stage and prod environments
cd ../stage/
terraform init
terraform apply

cd ../prod/
terraform init
terraform apply
```

### Recommendation

**For small to medium-sized projects with a limited number of environments**, Terraform workspaces are often more straightforward and easier to manage. They allow you to keep your configuration files organized in one place while handling different environment settings through variables.

**For larger, complex projects or when you need significant customization per environment**, separate folders provide better isolation and flexibility. This approach is also beneficial if you have distinct teams working on different environments independently.

Ultimately, the choice depends on your specific project requirements, team size, and preference for organization and management practices.

turn_logs.user: Now for EKS, should I use managed node groups or self-managed autoscaling groups?
turn_logs.response:
Choosing between managed node groups and self-managed Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs) for Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) depends on several factors such as ease of management, cost considerations, and specific requirements. Here’s a detailed comparison to help you decide:

### Managed Node Groups

**Pros:**
1. **Ease of Management**: 
   - Easier to set up and manage.
   - No need to create or configure Auto Scaling Groups manually.

2. **Integration with IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)**:
   - Simplifies the process of attaching IAM roles to Kubernetes service accounts, which is crucial for accessing AWS resources securely from within your cluster.

3. **Automatic Updates**:
   - Managed node groups automatically handle updates and lifecycle management of nodes.
   
4. **Monitoring and Logging**:
   - Easier integration with monitoring and logging services provided by AWS (e.g., CloudWatch).

5. **Cost Transparency**:
   - Cost breakdown is more straightforward as it’s integrated directly into the EKS console.

6. **Security Best Practices**:
   - Managed node groups often adhere to best practices for security, such as using IAM roles with least privilege principles.

### Cons:
1. **Limited Customization**:
   - Less flexibility in customizing node configurations and lifecycle management.
   
2. **Cost Overhead**:
   - May incur additional costs due to the managed nature of the service, although AWS pricing is generally competitive.

3. **Resource Constraints**:
   - Limited control over underlying EC2 instances (e.g., instance types, availability zones).

### Self-Managed Auto Scaling Groups

**Pros:**
1. **Full Control and Customization**:
   - Full control over node configurations, including instance types, security groups, IAM roles, etc.
   
2. **Flexibility in Lifecycle Management**:
   - More granular control over the lifecycle of nodes (e.g., custom scripts for initialization).

3. **Cost Optimization**:
   - Better ability to optimize costs by choosing specific instance types and configurations that fit your workload.

4. **Advanced Features**:
   - Can leverage advanced features like Spot Instances, mixed instances policies, etc., to reduce costs further.

5. **Custom Monitoring and Logging**:
   - Easier to integrate custom monitoring and logging solutions if needed.

### Cons:
1. **Complexity in Setup and Management**:
   - More complex setup process compared to managed node groups.
   
2. **Manual Maintenance**:
   - Requires manual management of Auto Scaling Groups, including updates and lifecycle hooks.

3. **IAM Role Integration**:
   - Manual integration with IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA) is more cumbersome.

4. **Learning Curve**:
   - Steeper learning curve due to the need to understand both EKS and EC2/Auto Scaling Group configurations.

### Recommendations

- **For Development, Testing, or Small Clusters**: 
  - Managed node groups are generally sufficient and easier to manage.
  
- **For Production Environments with Advanced Requirements**:
  - Self-managed Auto Scaling Groups offer more flexibility and control over costs and configurations. This is especially useful if you need advanced features like Spot Instances or mixed instance policies.

### Example Configuration

#### Managed Node Group (Example)

```hcl
module "eks" {
  source              = "./modules/eks"
  vpc_id              = module.network.vpc_id
  subnets             = module.network.public_subnets
  cluster_name        = var.cluster_name
  node_group_name     = var.node_group_name
  desired_size        = var.desired_size
}
```

#### Self-Managed Auto Scaling Group (Example)

```hcl
resource "aws_launch_template" "eks_node" {
  name_prefix           = "${var.cluster_name}-node"
  instance_type         = var.instance_type
  key_name              = var.key_name
  iam_instance_profile  = aws_iam_instance_profile.eks_node.id
  vpc_security_group_ids = [module.security.security_groups[0]]
  
  # Other configurations like user_data, block_device_mappings, etc.
}

resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "eks_nodes" {
  name_prefix           = "${var.cluster_name}-nodes"
  max_size              = var.max_size
  min_size              = var.min_size
  desired_capacity      = var.desired_size
  launch_template       = aws_launch_template.eks_node.id

  vpc_zone_identifier   = module.network.public_subnets
}

resource "aws_iam_instance_profile" "eks_node" {
  name_prefix           = "${var.cluster_name}-node"
  roles                 = [aws_iam_role.eks_node.id]
}
```

### Conclusion

If you prioritize ease of management and integration with AWS services, **managed node groups** are the way to go. For more control over configurations and cost optimization, **self-managed Auto Scaling Groups** offer a better fit.

Given your context, starting with managed node groups might be simpler initially, especially if you're setting up EKS for development or testing purposes. As you scale and need more customization, transitioning to self-managed ASGs can provide the flexibility required for production environments.

turn_logs.user: I need cluster add-ons too: metrics server, EBS CSI, and external DNS. Terraform or Helm after cluster bootstrap?
turn_logs.response:
When setting up Kubernetes cluster add-ons such as Metrics Server, EBS CSI Driver, and External DNS, you have two primary options:

1. **Terraform**: Use Terraform to deploy these components directly.
2. **Helm**: Use Helm charts to manage the deployment of these components after the cluster is bootstrapped.

Both approaches have their pros and cons. Let's explore each option in detail:

### Option 1: Using Terraform

**Pros:**
- **Centralized Management**: All infrastructure (VPC, EKS, add-ons) can be managed within a single Terraform configuration.
- **Version Control**: Everything is version-controlled, making it easier to track changes and rollbacks.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity**: Managing Kubernetes components directly with Terraform can become complex due to the need for custom resource definitions (CRDs), YAML files, etc.
- **Maintenance Overhead**: Keeping up-to-date with new versions of add-ons might require manual intervention in your Terraform scripts.

### Option 2: Using Helm

**Pros:**
- **Simplicity and Standardization**: Helm charts are designed specifically for Kubernetes components, making it easier to manage and update them.
- **Community Support**: Many popular add-ons have official or community-maintained Helm charts available.
- **Ease of Update**: Updating add-ons is straightforward with Helm.

**Cons:**
- **Separate Management**: You need to manage your Terraform configuration for infrastructure and Helm for Kubernetes components separately.
- **Learning Curve**: If you're not familiar with Helm, there's an initial learning curve.

### Recommended Approach

Given the complexity of managing Kubernetes components directly in Terraform, using Helm is generally a better approach. Here’s how you can set up these add-ons after your EKS cluster has been bootstrapped:

#### Step 1: Install Helm and Tiller (if needed)

First, ensure that Helm is installed on your local machine or CI/CD pipeline.

```sh
# Install Helm
curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/master/scripts/get-helm-3 | bash

# Initialize Helm
helm init --upgrade
```

#### Step 2: Add Required Repositories

Add the repositories for the required add-ons:

```sh
helm repo add stable https://charts.helm.sh/stable
helm repo update
```

For EBS CSI Driver, you might need to add a specific repository:

```sh
helm repo add aws-controllers-chartrepo https://aws.github.io/eks-charts/
helm repo update
```

#### Step 3: Install Metrics Server

Install the Metrics Server using Helm:

```sh
helm install metrics-server stable/metrics-server --namespace kube-system
```

#### Step 4: Install EBS CSI Driver

Install the EBS CSI driver using Helm:

```sh
helm install aws-ebs-csi-driver aws-controllers-chartrepo/aws-ebs-csi-driver --namespace kube-system
```

#### Step 5: Install External DNS

Install External DNS to manage your Kubernetes services with Route 53 or other DNS providers:

```sh
helm install external-dns stable/external-dns --set serviceAccount.create=true --set rbac.create=true --set provider=aws --set aws.zoneType=public --namespace kube-system
```

### Example Terraform Configuration

If you still prefer to integrate Helm into your Terraform setup, you can use the `helm` resource provided by the `terraform-providers/helm` module.

#### Step 1: Add Helm Provider in Terraform

Add the Helm provider to your Terraform configuration:

```hcl
provider "helm" {
  kubernetes {
    config_path = "~/.kube/config"
  }
}
```

#### Step 2: Install Metrics Server Using Helm

Install Metrics Server using a Helm release resource:

```hcl
resource "helm_release" "metrics_server" {
  name       = "metrics-server"
  repository = "https://charts.helm.sh/stable"
  chart      = "metrics-server"

  set {
    name  = "createTokenRelayService.enabled"
    value = true
  }

  set {
    name  = "serviceAccount.create"
    value = true
  }
}
```

#### Step 3: Install EBS CSI Driver Using Helm

Install the EBS CSI driver using a Helm release resource:

```hcl
resource "helm_release" "aws_ebs_csi_driver" {
  name       = "aws-ebs-csi-driver"
  repository = "https://aws.github.io/eks-charts/"
  chart      = "aws-ebs-csi-driver"

  set {
    name  = "serviceAccount.create"
    value = true
  }
}
```

#### Step 4: Install External DNS Using Helm

Install External DNS using a Helm release resource:

```hcl
resource "helm_release" "external_dns" {
  name       = "external-dns"
  repository = "https://charts.helm.sh/stable"
  chart      = "external-dns"

  set {
    name  = "serviceAccount.create"
    value = true
  }

  set {
    name  = "rbac.create"
    value = true
  }

  set {
    name  = "provider"
    value = "aws"
  }

  set {
    name  = "aws.zoneType"
    value = "public"
  }
}
```

### Conclusion

Using Helm to manage Kubernetes add-ons is generally more straightforward and better supported by the community. However, if you prefer a single Terraform configuration for all your infrastructure needs, integrating Helm into Terraform as shown above can be an effective solution.

Given the ease of use and maintenance benefits provided by Helm, it's recommended to use Helm for installing cluster add-ons after bootstrapping your EKS cluster.
